questionText: Disprove the statement: ``The sum of any three consecutive integers is even.''

steps.description: Read the claim as a universal statement.
steps.explanation: The statement claims the sum is always even; one set of three consecutive integers with an odd sum disproves it.
We need just one starting integer for which the sum is odd.
We test the three consecutive integers $1,2,3$.
Their sum is $6$, which is even — so this particular case supports the claim.
We now test $2,3,4$ to see whether the pattern really always holds.
Their sum is $9$ — an odd total, unlike the first case, so the pattern does not always hold.
Since $9$ is odd, this set of three consecutive integers has an odd sum.
The integers $2,3,4$ sum to $9$, which is odd, so the claim `the sum is always even' is disproved.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $2+3+4 = 9$, which is odd, so the sum of three consecutive integers is not always even. The statement is false. $\blacksquare$
